name: build-constraint-on-injector
description: Tests that the model adds //go:build wireinject to injector files to prevent duplicate-symbol compile errors
prompt:
I'm setting up google/wire in my Go project. Here's my injector file:

```go
package main

import "github.com/google/wire"

func InitApp() (*App, error) {
    wire.Build(InfraSet, ServiceSet, NewApp)
    return nil, nil
}
```

Wire generates wire_gen.go successfully, but when I run `go build`, I get a 'redeclared in this block' compile error for InitApp. What's wrong and how do I fix it?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest renaming the function, reorganizing packages, or not identify that the missing //go:build wireinject tag is the cause — both the stub and wire_gen.go define InitApp, causing the duplicate.

assertions.text: Identifies the missing //go:build wireinject build tag as the root cause
Shows //go:build wireinject as the first line of the injector file
Explains that the tag prevents the stub from being compiled into the binary (only wire_gen.go compiles)
Does NOT suggest renaming the function or reorganizing packages as the fix
Does NOT suggest deleting wire_gen.go as the fix

name: interface-binding-required
description: Tests that wire.Bind is required for interface-to-concrete mappings and cannot be inferred
trap: Without the skill, the model might suggest wire should automatically resolve the interface, or suggest wrapping NewUserRepo to return UserStore directly, missing the explicit wire.Bind requirement.

assertions.text: Explains that wire never auto-resolves interface satisfaction — bindings must be explicit
Shows wire.Bind(new(UserStore), new(*PostgresUserRepo)) added to the provider set
Places wire.Bind inside the same wire.NewSet (or adds it to a set in wire.Build)
Explains WHY wire requires explicit bindings (predictability — avoids surprise rebinding when new implementations are added)
Does NOT suggest changing NewUserRepo to return UserStore directly as the primary fix

name: duplicate-type-named-wrapper
description: Tests the named-type pattern to disambiguate multiple values of the same underlying type
trap: Without the skill, the model might suggest using wire.Value or provider arguments, or use a config struct — missing the idiomatic named-type wrapper pattern that wire's own docs recommend.

assertions.text: Introduces distinct named types (e.g., type PrimaryDSN string and type ReplicaDSN string)
Updates NewPrimaryDSN to return PrimaryDSN and NewReplicaDSN to return ReplicaDSN
Updates NewPrimaryDB and NewReplicaDB signatures to accept the named types
Explains that wire enforces one provider per type, so distinct named types are the correct solution
Does NOT suggest using a single Config struct with both DSNs as the primary fix (that avoids the problem rather than solving it with named types)

name: cleanup-signature
description: Tests the (T, func(), error) cleanup provider pattern instead of manual defer in main
trap: Without the skill, the model might suggest passing db out of InitApp as a second return value, or storing it as a global, missing the (T, func(), error) cleanup provider pattern.

assertions.text: Changes NewDB to return (*sql.DB, func(), error) where the cleanup function calls db.Close()
Changes the injector function to return (*App, func(), error) to propagate the cleanup chain
Shows main calling defer cleanup() after the nil-check
Explains that wire chains cleanup functions and calls them in reverse construction order
Does NOT suggest passing db as an extra return value from InitApp alongside *App

name: no-edit-wire-gen
description: Tests that the model never edits wire_gen.go and instructs re-running wire ./... instead
prompt:
I added a new *Logger parameter to my NewServer constructor in my google/wire project:

```go
func NewServer(db *sql.DB, log *zap.Logger) *Server { ... }
```

Now `go build` fails with 'too few arguments in call to NewServer'. The error is inside wire_gen.go on line 47. Should I edit wire_gen.go to add the logger argument there, or is there another way?
trap: Without the skill, a model may suggest editing wire_gen.go directly to 'fix' the build error quickly, which would be overwritten on the next wire run.

assertions.text: Explicitly says NOT to edit wire_gen.go (it is always overwritten)
Instructs running wire ./... to regenerate wire_gen.go
Explains that *zap.Logger must be provided in the graph (either via a provider or wire.Value)
Shows how to add NewLogger (or wire.Value) to the appropriate wire.NewSet so the dependency is satisfied
Does NOT present editing wire_gen.go as an option

name: provider-set-organization
description: Tests per-package provider set organization instead of one giant set in main
prompt:
My Go service using google/wire is growing. I currently have everything in one place:

```go
// wire.go
//go:build wireinject

func InitApp() (*App, func(), error) {
    wire.Build(
        NewConfig, NewDB, NewCache, NewLogger,
        NewUserRepo, NewOrderRepo, NewProductRepo,
        wire.Bind(new(UserStore), new(*PostgresUserRepo)),
        wire.Bind(new(OrderStore), new(*PostgresOrderRepo)),
        wire.Bind(new(ProductStore), new(*PostgresProductRepo)),
        NewUserService, NewOrderService, NewProductService,
        NewHTTPServer, NewRouter,
        NewApp,
    )
    return nil, nil, nil
}
```

This is getting unwieldy. How should I organize this with google/wire?
trap: Without the skill, the model may just split the providers into helper variables in the same package, missing the idiomatic per-package wire.NewSet pattern.

assertions.text: Introduces per-package wire.NewSet variables (e.g., InfraSet, RepoSet, ServiceSet, TransportSet)
Each set lives in its own package's wire.go file (not all in main)
The injector wire.Build references the set variables rather than individual providers
wire.Bind declarations move into the relevant package's set (not into wire.Build directly)
Explains the benefit: per-package sets are independently composable and keep the injector readable

name: injector-parameter-vs-value-provider
description: Tests using wire.Value or injector parameters for pre-built values instead of wrapper constructors
prompt:
In my Go app using google/wire, I parse a *Config struct from command-line flags in main() before calling InitApp. I tried writing a no-op provider:

```go
var parsedCfg *Config

func ProvideConfig() *Config { return parsedCfg }

var AppSet = wire.NewSet(ProvideConfig, ...)
```

This works but feels wrong — I'm using a global variable. Is there a cleaner way to pass a pre-built *Config into the wire graph?
trap: Without the skill, the model might suggest keeping the global variable pattern or using init(), missing both wire.Value and the injector-parameter patterns.

assertions.text: Shows the injector-parameter approach: func InitApp(cfg *Config) (*App, func(), error) with wire.Build
OR shows wire.Value(cfg) inside wire.Build — both are valid answers
Explains that injector parameters are treated as pre-built providers by wire
Does NOT use a global variable as the recommended solution
Does NOT suggest using init() to set the value

name: fields-of-struct
description: Tests wire.FieldsOf to expose struct fields as individual graph nodes
trap: Without the skill, the model will suggest three named-type wrappers or three extraction functions, missing wire.FieldsOf which promotes struct fields directly.

assertions.text: Uses wire.FieldsOf(new(Config), "DatabaseDSN", "CacheAddress", "APIKey") or a subset
Places wire.FieldsOf inside the provider set or wire.Build
Updates NewDB, NewCache, NewExternalClient to accept the string fields as parameters (or uses named types alongside FieldsOf)
Explains that wire.FieldsOf promotes struct fields as individual graph nodes without manual extraction functions
Does NOT suggest writing three separate func GetDatabaseDSN(c *Config) string extractor functions as the primary recommendation

name: test-injector-pattern
description: Tests the test-injector pattern with wire.Bind for fake dependencies instead of runtime mocking hacks
prompt: My Go service is wired with google/wire. I have a Mailer interface implemented by SMTPMailer in production. I want integration tests that use a FakeMailer instead — recording sent emails — without modifying the production provider sets. The test must wire the full graph (not just NewUserService in isolation). How should I approach this?
trap: Without the skill, the model may suggest monkey-patching, a global variable for the mailer, or a runtime DI container for tests — missing the test-injector pattern with a test-only wire.NewSet and wire.Bind.

assertions.text: Creates a test-only provider set (e.g., TestMailerSet) with NewFakeMailer and wire.Bind(new(Mailer), new(*FakeMailer))
Creates a test injector function in a _test.go file with //go:build wireinject
The test injector's wire.Build composes the production sets with the test-only set
Does NOT suggest global variables, monkey-patching, or a runtime DI container for tests
Mentions that wire ./... (or go generate) must be run to produce the test-injector generated code

name: wire-vs-fx-for-daemon
description: Tests that the model recommends uber-go/fx over wire for long-running services that need lifecycle management
prompt: I'm starting a new Go HTTP server project and evaluating DI options. A colleague suggested google/wire because 'it's simpler and type-safe at compile time.' The server needs graceful shutdown (drain in-flight requests), OnStart/OnStop hooks for the database pool and metrics exporter, and should handle SIGINT/SIGTERM. Should I use wire?
trap: Without the skill, the model may agree that wire is suitable because it's simple and compile-time safe, not recognizing that lifecycle, signal handling, and hook ordering are exactly what fx provides and wire explicitly lacks.

assertions.text: Identifies that wire has no built-in lifecycle management (no OnStart/OnStop hooks)
Identifies that wire has no built-in signal handling (SIGINT/SIGTERM)
Recommends uber-go/fx (or at minimum flags it as the better fit) for a long-running HTTP daemon with lifecycle needs
Does NOT recommend wire as sufficient for a service requiring graceful shutdown and lifecycle hooks
Mentions that with wire the developer must implement shutdown and signal handling manually
